narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of past students, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to forecast the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 31, middle family income bracket, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 4th, and your plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 48% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger predicted odds of bar passage to maintain high success rates. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA emerged as the most influential positive element, actually working strongly in your favor by suggesting a solid foundation that pushes toward passing the bar, especially since your 3.40 exceeds the typical 3.21 average. However, your LSAT score ranked as the second-most impactful factor and pulled in the opposite direction, contributing toward the failure prediction because your 31 is notably below the dataset average of 36.70, signaling potential challenges in the rigorous reasoning demands of law school and the bar. Your middle family income bracket came in third, also nudging the prediction toward failure compared to higher-income peers who often have more resources for preparation. While your full-time enrollment aligns with the vast majority of students (over 92%), and your school deciles around the middle of the pack are not standout concerns, they didn't outweigh the key drags from LSAT and income. Overall, your profile stacks up as competent in GPA but hampered by a lower LSAT relative to typical admits, placing you in a riskier category for first-time bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student—many from similar backgrounds excel with targeted improvements— but a data-driven call by the model. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.70 average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening study habits or seeking fee waivers and prep resources tailored for middle-income students might also help, and reapplying after demonstrable gains in these areas could open doors at this or other programs. You're clearly capable, given your GPA strength, so focusing here positions you well for success.
